title: Economic Fluctuations and Growth
gist: This is an NBER research-AREA listing page for the Economic Fluctuations and Growth program, not a single paper: it enumerates dozens of recent working papers with one-sentence abstracts. The most notable individual findings cluster around dollar/reserve-currency erosion, AI and data as economic inputs, US productivity and labor-supply puzzles, and monetary-policy transmission.

insights.claim: Losing US reserve-currency status would cost roughly 1% of GDP per annum in lost seigniorage, forcing American households to spend less and a real depreciation to clear excess US goods, per NBER WP 35328 by Jiang, Krishnamurthy, Lustig & Richmond.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the tail-risk cost of de-dollarization for USD assets and US consumption — a level to watch as dollar-alternative narratives grow.
insights.quote: the loss of seigniorage (1% of GDP per annum) makes American households spend less, mostly on U.S. goods, leaving an excess supply of U.S. goods to be cleared by a real depreciation
insights.locator: Dollar Erosion: Understanding the Loss of Reserve Currency Status

insights.claim: Dollar debt dominance becomes fragile when borrowing countries hold dollar-denominated liabilities but increasingly earn yuan-linked revenues, creating a currency mismatch, per NBER WP 35272 by Benguria, Rojas & Saffie.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Frames a mechanism for EM sovereign-debt stress and a potential channel for yuan internationalization.
insights.quote: countries have inherited dollar liabilities but increasingly earn yuan-linked revenues
insights.locator: Geopolitical Fragmentation, Sovereign Debt, and Dollar Dominance

insights.claim: Convenience yields on the US dollar and on US Treasury securities have diverged markedly since the early 2010s, decoupling 'dollar privilege' from 'Treasury privilege', per Du, Keerati & Schreger (NBER WP 35000).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Suggests safe-asset demand for Treasuries and for dollar cash can move apart — relevant to Treasury term-premium and funding-market analysis.
insights.quote: a pronounced divergence between the convenience yields on the US dollar and US Treasury securities since the early 2010s
insights.locator: Convenience Yields: The US Dollar vs. US Treasuries

insights.claim: Japan's public sector borrowed mainly at floating rates while investing in longer-duration risky assets, earning about 6.25% of GDP per year above its below-market funding costs over the past decade, per Chien, Cole & Lustig.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the sovereign carry trade embedded in Japanese public finances — a vulnerability if rates or risk assets reverse.
insights.quote: earning an annual return of 6.25 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) above its below-market funding costs
insights.locator: What About Japan?

themes.theme: AI, data, and compute as economic inputs

insights.claim: Measured AI exposure overstates actual workplace AI adoption; the paper proposes a new adoption index based on comparative advantage, validated on the German DiWaBe survey, per Lindenlaub, Oh, Rodriguez & Veldkamp (WP 35271).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Cautions against using AI-exposure scores to predict real productivity/adoption — relevant to AI-winner stock screens.
insights.quote: the gap between measures of AI exposure and measures of AI adoption in the workplace
insights.locator: Beyond Exposure: Predicting AI Adoption Based on Comparative Advantage

insights.claim: Data is an intangible asset largely missed by national accounts and firm balance sheets, so its investment and depreciation go unmeasured, per Bhutani, Ordoñez & Veldkamp's 'The Missing Value of Data' (WP 35266).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Implies official productivity/GDP statistics understate the data economy — a measurement gap behind productivity-puzzle debates.
insights.quote: investments in data are missed in national accounts, and depreciation losses are missed in firms balance sheets
insights.locator: The Missing Value of Data

insights.claim: Rapid AI diffusion is accelerating data-center investment and raising concerns about local economic and other effects, studied via a shift-share approach by Alvarez, Argente, Chow & Van Patten (WP 35194).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Frames data-center buildout as a measurable local economic shock — relevant to power, real estate, and AI-infra theses.
insights.quote: The rapid diffusion of artificial intelligence (AI) is intensifying demand for compute, accelerating investment in data centers
insights.locator: Data Centers and Local Economies in the Age of AI

insights.claim: AI algorithms, software, and hardware can cut structural estimation of dynamic equilibrium models from days to minutes by treating prices as pseudo-parameters and market clearing as moment conditions, per Duarte & Fonseca (WP 35283).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Signals AI is reshaping the economics research toolkit itself, not just being studied as an object.
insights.quote: reduces estimation time from days to minutes
insights.locator: AI for Structural Estimation

insights.claim: A graph-based deep-learning model on security-level intermediary holdings data could improve macroprudential financial regulation, per Clayton & Coppola's 'The Optimal Use of AI in Financial Regulation' (WP 35227).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Points to AI-driven supervision as an emerging regulatory tool affecting financial firms' holdings disclosure.
insights.locator: The Optimal Use of AI in Financial Regulation

insights.claim: US manufacturing productivity growth collapsed from +3.3% annually in 1987-2010 to -0.3% from 2010 to 2023, and Gordon & Ryu argue the puzzle actually starts a decade before 2010 (WP 35285).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: A hard data point on the depth of the US manufacturing productivity slump — central to long-run growth and reshoring debates.
insights.quote: evaporating from an annual rate of +3.3 percent during 1987-2010 to -0.3 percent from 2010 to 2023
insights.locator: Does the Import Invasion Explain the Mysterious Disappearance of Productivity Growth in U.S. Manufacturing?

insights.claim: Flat US productivity growth despite rising R&D may reflect growth getting harder to find rather than ideas getting harder to find, per Fort, Goldschlag, Liang, Schott & Zolas using a 45-year firm patenting panel (WP 35182).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'ideas are getting harder to find' consensus — implications for how to value R&D-intensive firms.
insights.quote: Growth is Getting Harder to Find, Not Ideas
insights.locator: Growth is Getting Harder to Find, Not Ideas

insights.claim: Falling transportation costs over the past century yielded substantial economic benefits, quantified for the US 1947-2017 by Coşar, Osotimehin & Popov.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Provides a long-run baseline for the growth contribution of logistics — context for current supply-chain and freight dynamics.
insights.quote: transportation costs have fallen dramatically, yielding substantial economic benefits
insights.locator: Transportation Productivity and the US Economy, 1947–2017

insights.claim: The 50-year decline in US male labor force participation is partly caused by men's beliefs about the returns to work, shaped by lifetime experiences of the aggregate male labor market, per Levin & Vidart (WP 35327).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Adds a belief/experience channel to the structural labor-supply decline behind tight-but-shrinking workforce dynamics.
insights.quote: changes in men's beliefs about the returns to work, shaped by lifetime experiences of the aggregate male labor market
insights.locator: 'Nobody Wants to Work Anymore'

insights.claim: Small positive micro Marshallian labor-supply elasticities can be reconciled with the strongly negative macro wage-hours relationship using a single utility function, per Karabarbounis (WP 35329).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Resolves a long-standing micro-macro contradiction relevant to potential-output and slack estimates.
insights.quote: Micro estimates of the Marshallian elasticity of labor supply are small and typically positive, whereas cross-country and time-series patterns of hours imply a strong negative relationship
insights.locator: Reconciling Micro Elasticities with the Macro Decline in Labor Supply

insights.claim: The canonical employer-size wage effect disappears completely across establishments within the same firm, even in different local labor markets, per Diegmann, Müller & Schoefer (WP 35265).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Challenges a basic labor-economics fact, with implications for monopsony and wage-setting models.
insights.quote: this canonical fact disappears completely across establishments within the same firm
insights.locator: Off the Labor Supply Curve: The Zero Employer Size Wage Effect Within Large Firms

insights.claim: Firms with high monopsony power (those accounting for over 10% of the local wage bill) respond less to monetary policy in their wage bill, dampening transmission, per Bardóczy, Bornstein & Salgado using US Census data (WP 35335).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Implies labor-market concentration weakens the wage channel of monetary policy — relevant to Fed transmission assumptions.
insights.quote: firms with high monopsony powerdefined as those accounting for over 10 percent of the local wage billrespond less to monetary policy
insights.locator: Monopsony Power and the Transmission of Monetary Policy

insights.claim: Quantitative easing should be judged mainly on achieving core macro objectives and its effect on the consolidated fiscal position, not solely blamed for post-COVID inflation and central-bank losses, per Adrian, Erceg, Kolasa, Lindé & Zabczyk (WP 35297).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the dominant critique of QE — relevant to expectations for future balance-sheet policy.
insights.quote: QE should be evaluated mainly on its ability to achieve core macro-objectives as well for its effects on the consolidated fiscal position
insights.locator: Macroeconomic and Fiscal Consequences of Quantitative Easing

insights.claim: The aggregate consumption response to a fiscal transfer is strongly concave in its size because MPCs are highly non-linear in wealth, so larger transfers have proportionally smaller effects, per Bianchi & Kaplan (WP 35311).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Implies diminishing bang-for-buck of large stimulus checks — relevant to sizing fiscal-impulse forecasts.
insights.quote: the aggregate consumption response to a fiscal transfer is strongly concave in its size
insights.locator: How Small is Small? Non-linearities in Heterogeneous Agent Models

insights.claim: Pessimistic news about France, Italy and Spain's debt-to-GDP ratios raises German firms' debt beliefs and lifts their one- and three-year inflation expectations, evidence of fiscal-dominance fears in a monetary union, per Bianchi, Faccini, Melosi & Wehrhöfer (WP 35341).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Shows sovereign-debt worries can spill into inflation expectations across a currency union — a euro-area inflation risk channel.
insights.quote: Pessimistic news raises debt beliefs and increases one- and three-year
insights.locator: Lack of Trust and Fiscal Dominance

insights.claim: When upstream input costs rise, similar absolute price increases hit cheap and expensive varieties alike, but the same dollar increase is a larger percentage change for low-price goods, generating 'cheapflation' that worsens inflation inequality, per Sangani (WP 35235).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Explains why cost-push shocks hit lower-income consumers harder — relevant to distributional inflation narratives.
insights.quote: the same absolute price increase constitutes a larger percentage change for low-price products
insights.locator: Cheapflation Cycles

insights.claim: Mortgage rate lock-in drove a 40% drop in US existing home sales between 2022 and 2024 by reducing both supply (households not selling) and demand (households not buying elsewhere), per Fonseca, Liu & Mabille (WP 35237).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies how rate lock-in froze the housing market — key for housing-turnover and homebuilder analysis.
insights.quote: evidenced by a 40% drop in U.S. existing home sales between 2022 and 2024
insights.locator: Unlocking Mortgage Lock-In

insights.claim: Global supply-chain disruptions alter monetary-policy transmission through logistical bottlenecks where upstream slack coexists with downstream shortages, per Bai, Fernández-Villaverde, Li & Zanetti (WP 35209).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Suggests central-bank policy works differently during supply-chain stress — relevant to post-pandemic policy modeling.
insights.quote: logistical bottlenecks (upstream slack coexisting with downstream shortages)
insights.locator: State Dependence of Monetary Policy During Global Supply Chain Disruptions

insights.claim: Macroeconomic beliefs are shaped by context and emotions that influence which past experiences people recall, an 'animal spirits' mechanism tested by priming Dutch National Bank Survey respondents, per Bordalo, Gennaioli, Shleifer et al. (WP 35214).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Offers a memory-and-emotion model of sentiment swings — relevant to forecasting consumer/business confidence shifts.
insights.quote: context and emotions affect macroeconomic beliefs by shaping which experiences people recall
insights.locator: The Psychology of Macroeconomic Expectations

insights.claim: The Campbell-Shiller (1988) log-linear approximation, widely treated as a model-free accounting identity, does not always hold in sample, in expectation, or under arbitrary subjective beliefs, per Ben-David & Chinco (WP 35189).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Undercuts a foundational asset-pricing identity used to decompose returns — a caution for return-decomposition research.
insights.quote: None of these claims is true. The formula is far from automatic even in realized data.
insights.locator: Crimes Against Campbell-Shiller

insights.claim: Decentralized automation raises capital's share of national income and, by increasing capital-labor inequality, can raise the likelihood of worker revolt in Acemoglu, Gitmez & Shadmehr's model of automation and repression (WP 35336).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Links automation-driven inequality to political instability — a societal tail-risk of the AI/automation wave.
insights.quote: greater inequality between capital and labor increases the likelihood of a revolt
insights.locator: Automation and Repression

insights.claim: Great powers like the US and China use existing trade and financial linkages to exert geoeconomic coercion on both foreign governments and foreign private firms, per Clayton, Maggiori & Schreger (WP 35343).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Provides a framework for who gets targeted by economic statecraft — relevant to sanctions and supply-chain risk.
insights.quote: Great powers, like the United States and China, use existing trade and financial linkages of their economies to the rest of the world to exert geoeconomic power
insights.locator: The Targets of Geoeconomic Coercion

insights.claim: Uncertainty about US student-loan forgiveness (announced in 2022 but never implemented) measurably affected households' beliefs and their borrowing, spending and debt-payment behavior, per Koustas, Weber & Yannelis (WP 35319).
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Shows announced-but-undelivered policy still moves consumer behavior — a channel for tracking policy-driven spending.
insights.quote: which following electoral promises, was announced in 2022 but never
insights.locator: Beliefs and Actions under Government Policy Uncertainty
